documentStatus.evidence: Page is live and states 'These guidelines are updated periodically'

policyAuthority.evidence: Page title and content are from HUIT; references Harvard's Information Security and Data Privacy office

aiServiceStatus.evidence: The University supports responsible experimentation with generative AI tools, but there are important considerations

serviceTreatment.evidence: Level 2 and above confidential data must only be entered into generative AI tools that have been assessed and approved for such use by Harvard's Information Security and Data Privacy office

governanceThemes.evidence: You should not enter data classified as confidential (Level 2 and above) into publicly-available generative AI tools
AI-generated content can be inaccurate, misleading, or entirely fabricated (sometimes called 'hallucinations')
References local academic and administrative policies; many Schools have developed or updated policies around use of generative AI in the classroom
All vendor generative AI tools must be assessed for risk by Harvard's Information Security and Data Privacy office prior to use
Generative AI has made it easier for malicious actors to create sophisticated phishing emails and deepfakes

audience.evidence: References faculty, students, staff; mentions School or Unit policies; applies to Harvard work broadly

bannedServices.value: Not mentioned — no specific AI services are banned
bannedServices.evidence: Page references approved tools list at /ai/tools but does not name banned services
